_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: Sets
summary: Keep one copy of each value and compare groups directly.
refresher: A set is an unordered collection of unique values. Add with `add`, remove safely with `discard`, and use union, intersection, or difference to compare groups. Empty sets use `set()`, because `{}` creates a dictionary.

blocks.body: A set is useful when the question is about membership, not position. It can track the courses a student has completed or the tags attached to an article. Duplicate input collapses into one stored value.

blocks.caption: Repeated values disappear when the list becomes a set. The result has three unique course codes even though the source list has four items.
blocks.code:
courses = ["CS101", "MATH120", "CS101", "ENG101"]
unique_courses = set(courses)
print(len(unique_courses))
print("CS101" in unique_courses)

blocks.body: Sets do not promise an index order, so `skills[0]` is not valid. If order matters, keep a list. If fast membership and uniqueness matter, a set is usually the clearer tool.

blocks.caption: `remove` raises KeyError when the value is absent. `discard` leaves the set unchanged, which is convenient when absence is acceptable.

blocks.title: Curly braces have one empty exception
blocks.body: `{"Python"}` is a set, but `{}` is an empty dictionary. Write `set()` when you need an empty set, then add values to it.

blocks.why: The `&` operator computes intersection, keeping only values present in both sets. The result remains a set, not a list.

blocks.body: Union combines both groups, intersection keeps the overlap, and difference keeps values found only on the left. Those operations often replace nested loops with a statement that matches the question directly.

blocks.prompt: Which expression finds courses completed by Ada but not Ben?
blocks.why: Set difference keeps members from the left set that are absent from the right. Here the result is `{"MATH120"}`.

blocks.title: Set checklist
items: Use a set when duplicates have no meaning.
Do not depend on item positions.
Prefer `discard` when a missing value is acceptable.
